How I turned a broad business proposal into a clear workflow, architecture, MVP scope, and implementation plan

6 min read
Just now

--

This case study is based on a real client engagement. Client names, locations, commercial figures, and identifying implementation details have been changed or omitted to protect confidentiality.
A client once sent me a business proposal and asked for an implementation estimate.
The document described a digital authorization and transaction platform involving multiple user roles, balance control, one-time authorizations, field validation, end-user confirmation, reporting, and integration with an existing operational system.
At first glance, it looked like a pricing request.
But before giving a number, I realized that the real question was not:
How much will this cost?
The real question was:
What exactly are we building, and how should the system work?
So instead of immediately sending a quotation, I created a technical and architectural draft.
That document turned a broad business idea into a buildable backend plan.
The real challenge was not coding
The proposal explained many desired features, but several engineering decisions were still unclear:

• What was the core business entity?

• How should the end-to-end workflow behave?

• When should value be reserved, settled, or released?

• Which role could perform each action?

• What should happen when confirmation failed?

• Could an authorization be reused?

• What belonged in the MVP?

• What should be postponed?

• Which risks could materially change the estimate?
Without answering these questions, any time or cost estimate would have been based on assumptions.
The first task was therefore not implementation.
It was clarification.
Finding the core business entity
I began by identifying the object that connected the whole workflow.
This object represented a one-time authorization linked to:

• the organization funding the transaction

• the person allowed to use it

• the related asset

• the permitted service location

• the maximum authorized value

• the validity period

• the final transaction record
Once this core entity was defined, the rest of the system became easier to structure.
It answered one central question:
Who is allowed to use how much value, under what conditions, and where?
That single decision simplified identity, authorization, accounting, security, and audit requirements.
Mapping the workflow from end to end
I then translated the proposal into a complete operational flow:

• An organization reserves value for an authorization.

• A digital authorization is created and issued.

• An operator validates it at the service location.

• The actual transaction amount is entered.

• The end user confirms or rejects the transaction.

• The system settles the used amount.

• Any unused amount is released.

• The final transaction and audit record are stored.
Mapping the full workflow exposed questions that were easy to miss in the original proposal:

• What happens if the user rejects the transaction?

• What happens when the authorization expires?

• Can the same authorization be used twice?

• How should unused value be returned?

• What happens if two requests try to use the same balance?

• Which actions require authenticated staff?
These were not secondary implementation details.
They were core business rules.
Turning the workflow into a state machine
The workflow was converted into explicit lifecycle states:
Created → Issued → Validated → Pending Confirmation → Completed / Cancelled / Expired
Completed, cancelled, and expired were defined as terminal states.
That meant the same authorization could not be reused after reaching a final outcome.
The state machine gave the system:

• predictable behavior

• clearer validation rules

• simpler API design

• stronger auditability

• easier testing

• protection against repeated or invalid actions
Instead of letting every endpoint make isolated decisions, the system now had a controlled lifecycle.
Separating available and reserved value
Because the workflow involved monetary authorization, one balance field was not enough.
I proposed separating value into:

• available balance

• reserved balance
When an authorization was created, the amount moved from available to reserved.
When the transaction was confirmed:

• the actual used amount was settled

• the unused amount was returned
When the authorization was cancelled or expired:

• the full reserved amount was released
This made the financial behavior easier to understand and reduced the risk of double usage or inconsistent balances.
Defining roles and accountability
The proposal involved several participants, but their permissions needed to be explicit.
I separated responsibilities across roles such as:

• system administrator

• organization administrator

• location administrator

• operator

• end user
Each important action was tied to a specific role.
The system needed to record:

• who created the authorization

• who validated it

• who entered the transaction

• who confirmed it

• when each state transition occurred
This turned a functional workflow into an auditable business system.
Reducing the scope to a real MVP
The original proposal included reporting, analytics, integrations, risk control, dashboards, and future enhancements.
Building all of them at once would have increased cost, timeline, and delivery risk.
So I reduced the first phase to one complete value chain:
value available → authorization created → validation → confirmation → settlement → transaction recorded
The MVP deliberately excluded advanced analytics, complex fraud detection, extensive reporting, and UI refinements.
The goal was not to build every possible feature.
The goal was to prove one complete, reliable, and auditable workflow.
Turning the architecture into a delivery plan
Once the workflow and MVP were clear, I divided the system into practical milestones:

• Core data model, authentication, and role-based access

• Authorization creation and balance reservation

• Validation and pending transaction workflow

• Confirmation, settlement, refund handling, and finalization

• Limited reporting and operational improvements
For each milestone, I defined:

• backend deliverables

• frontend dependencies

• workflow coverage

• expected duration

• explicit exclusions
This made the estimate more realistic and reduced the chance of scope misunderstandings later.
Identifying risks before development
The review also surfaced several questions that needed agreement before coding:

• single-use versus reusable authorization

• value-based versus quantity-based control

• identity verification

• replay risks

• operator accountability

• offline behavior

• confirmation failures

• concurrency safety

• integration boundaries

• scope expansion
Each of these decisions could affect the architecture, timeline, or budget.
Finding them early was part of the value of the review.
The outcome
The client had originally asked for a quotation.
What they received was a structured technical and architectural draft that turned a broad proposal into:

• a clear domain model

• an end-to-end workflow

• a state machine

• a safe settlement model

• role and permission boundaries

• a focused MVP

• practical delivery milestones

• risks and clarification points

• a realistic basis for estimation
The draft gave the client a concrete foundation for discussing scope, implementation priorities, timeline, and cost.
The conversation changed from:
How much will this cost?
to:
What exactly are we building, how should it work, and how can we deliver it safely?
That was also the moment I recognized an important part of my own value.
I was not only implementing backend code.
I was helping turn unclear business ideas into systems that development teams could understand, estimate, and build.
What this case demonstrates
This is the kind of work I do best:

• reviewing business proposals and API documentation

• identifying the real workflow behind a feature list

• defining core entities and state transitions

• clarifying system and data boundaries

• identifying technical and operational risks before implementation

• reducing broad requirements into a focused MVP

• creating an implementation roadmap and delivery milestones

• producing technical documentation that both technical and non-technical stakeholders can follow

• continuing into backend implementation when required

Planning an API or workflow-heavy system?
For many integration and workflow projects, the biggest risk is not writing code.
It is beginning development before the workflow, data ownership, system boundaries, failure handling, and MVP scope are clear.
If your team has an API specification, business proposal, or operational workflow that still needs clarification, I can help turn it into a practical integration and implementation plan.
I offer small, clearly scoped API / Workflow Integration Reviews, with the option to continue into backend implementation afterward, depending on the project’s existing technology stack and needs.
Feel free to contact me through GitHub or LinkedIn.
